title: Plastic and Microplastic Pollution
focus: Characteristics, types, and impacts of microplastics, along with India's regulatory framework (PWM Rules).
rationale: Covers the foundational concepts of microplastics (primary vs secondary) and the specific regulatory timeline/thickness limits of India's Plastic Waste Management Rules, which are highly testable in UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Types: Microplastic (up to 5 millimetres in diameter); Nanoplastics (less than 1,000 nanometers)... Primary Microplastics: Intentionally manufactured. E.g. Plastic pellets, Microbeads... Secondary Microplastics: From breakdown of larger plastic items. E.g. Microfibers... PWM (Amendment) Rules, 2021: Raised thickness limit (50 microns to 75 microns starting September 30, 2021, and then to 120 microns from December 31, 2022) for carry bags.
evidenceBullets: Microplastics are up to 5 millimetres in diameter, while nanoplastics are less than 1,000 nanometers.
Primary microplastics are intentionally manufactured (e.g., microbeads in cosmetics), whereas secondary microplastics result from the breakdown of larger items (e.g., microfibers).
Microplastics reduce plants' photosynthetic ability by around 12% and cause biomagnification in marine life.
The Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 raised the thickness limit of plastic carry bags to 120 microns effective December 31, 2022.

title: Plastic-Related Chemical Terms and Bioremediation
focus: Specific polymers, plasticizers (Phthalates), and plastic-degrading bacteria in the news.
rationale: UPSC frequently asks about specific chemicals (like Phthalates, PBAT) and bioremediation agents (Ideonella sakaiensis) in the Science & Tech or Environment sections.
sectionExcerpt: Bacteria Ideonella sakaiensis: Plastic eating bacteria that can degrade PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)... Polybutylene Adipate Terephthalate (PBAT): Biodegradable, chemical-free thermoplastic copolymer made from renewable resources, like corn starch and sugarcane... Pthalates: Ortho-phthalates, or “phthalates,” are chemicals used in plastic products (commonly in specific type of plastic named polyvinyl chloride) to make the material soft and less brittle... Categorized as hazardous endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs); affect reproductive health.
evidenceBullets: Ideonella sakaiensis is a plastic-eating bacteria capable of degrading PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate).
PBAT is a biodegradable thermoplastic copolymer derived from renewable resources like corn starch and sugarcane.
Phthalates are plasticizers added to plastics like PVC to make them soft and less brittle, but they do not permanently bind to the plastic surfaces.
Phthalates are categorized as hazardous endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) that negatively affect reproductive health.

title: Urban Air Pollution and GRAP
focus: GRAP stages, IQAir report findings, WHO vs NAAQS standards, and India's mitigation initiatives.
rationale: Air pollution is a perennial UPSC topic. The specific AQI thresholds for GRAP stages, the comparison between WHO and NAAQS standards, and recent schemes (PM E-DRIVE, SATAT) are prime targets for objective questions.
sectionExcerpt: Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP)... Emergency response mechanism based on AQI level of Delhi implemented by CAQM... Stage 1: Poor category (AQI 201 to 300)... Stage 4: Severe + category (AQI 451+)... Causes: Winter weather conditions like temperature inversion; Narrow streets with tall buildings (street-canyon effect) trapping pollutants... Comparison of Air Quality Standards: PM2.5 WHO 2021 Guideline is 5 µg/m³, India (NAAQS) is 40 µg/m³... Steps taken to curb Air Pollution: Promotion of electric mobility under PM E-DRIVE Scheme... SATAT initiative for creating Compressed Bio-Gas (CBG) ecosystem.
evidenceBullets: GRAP is an emergency response mechanism implemented by CAQM, originally created on Supreme Court directions.
GRAP Stage 4 (Severe + category) is triggered when the AQI exceeds 451.
The street-canyon effect, caused by narrow streets with tall buildings, traps pollutants and exacerbates urban air pollution.
India's NAAQS limit for PM2.5 is 40 µg/m³, which is significantly higher than the WHO 2021 guideline of 5 µg/m³.
The SATAT initiative aims to create a Compressed Bio-Gas (CBG) ecosystem to help curb air pollution.
